title: The Disproportionate-Assets Case: A Continuing Story! (2)
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the serialized legal argument, exact dates and case identifiers, attributed allegations, genuine English judicial passages, Tamil explanations, numbered findings, directions, and the “To be continued” ending. The Tamil below is a source-checked derived reading copy in which only clear OCR character, spacing, malformed Unicode, and line-break errors are repaired. The canonical source remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: In yesterday's letter, I set out the opening portion of the Supreme Court's 2003 judgment on the petition filed by our General Secretary, the Professor. The succeeding portions of that judgment are as follows:

“S.S. Jawahar IAS, a former Deputy Secretary who had served as a government officer, testified as a prosecution witness in January 2003. He had also testified for the prosecution in 2000. When he was recalled in 2003 at the accused's instance, he claimed that his original testimony in 2000 had been given under compulsion. Yet the Public Prosecutor took no action when this witness turned hostile. Similarly, N.V. Balaji, the accused's auditor, gave evidence in 2000 that differed from what he said when recalled, but the Public Prosecutor ignored those contradictions.”

“Like them, several other witnesses, when recalled in the name of further examination, claimed that their original evidence had been given under compulsion. The Public Prosecutor did not treat any of them as hostile witnesses.”
“We have given a few examples of the witnesses who changed their testimony in this manner. They show the extent to which the prosecution acted in concert with the accused.”
The Supreme Court judgment further recorded:

“Counsel for the petitioner, the Professor, Mr Andhyarjuna, brought another matter to our attention. Referring to the examination of the first accused, Jayalalithaa, under Section 313, he submitted that it had been conducted outside lawful procedure. In an application filed on 24 February 2003, the first accused sought exemption from personal appearance and permission to answer questions through her lawyer. In paragraph five of the application, she stated that she had only just returned after a strenuous week-long election tour in Thoothukudi district. She was confined to bed with fever and had been advised by doctors to rest for several more days. She was therefore unable to appear personally in court for examination under Section 313. In paragraph six, she stated that the request was made not because of the office she held but because of her health. The Public Prosecutor did not oppose the application.”
In its judgment transferring Jayalalithaa's disproportionate-assets case from Chennai to Karnataka, the Supreme Court went on to make the following important observations:

“It is undisputed that 76 witnesses have been recalled. Many of them had earlier been cross-examined. We were informed that witnesses were recalled as senior counsel for Ms Jayalalithaa had been busy attending to some other case filed against her when they were first examined.”

Thus, seventy-six witnesses were recalled in this case, most of whom had already been cross-examined. When asked why, the explanation offered was that Jayalalithaa's senior counsel had been occupied appearing in several other cases against her when those witnesses were originally examined. Do you know what the Supreme Court judges said about recalling them in this way?

“The Bench said that the fact that witnesses were recalled for cross-examination on flimsy grounds after Ms Jayalalithaa assumed power as Chief Minister and the Public Prosecutor appointed by her Government did not oppose and/or gave consent to application for recall of witnesses was indicative of how ‘judicial process is being subverted.’”

In other words: after Jayalalithaa became Chief Minister, witnesses were recalled on some flimsy pretext. The Public Prosecutor appointed by her Government raised no objection and consented. This alone clearly showed the extent to which justice was being diverted in the case, the Supreme Court judges said.

“It does appear that the new Public Prosecutor is hand-in-glove with the accused thereby creating a reasonable apprehension of likelihood of failure of justice in the minds of the public at large. There is strong indication that the process of justice is being subverted.”

That is: the new Public Prosecutor was visibly acting in concert with the accused, Jayalalithaa's group. This created a reasonable apprehension in the public mind that justice would not be done. There was a strong indication that justice was being diverted.

When Jayalalithaa filed a frivolous application to avoid appearing in court for examination under Section 313, the Public Prosecutor did not object and merely watched. That was deplorable. “The conduct of the Public Prosecutor in not opposing such a frivolous application has to be deprecated.”

The Supreme Court judges strongly objected to the trial court dispensing with Jayalalithaa's personal appearance. They said: “Be you ever so high, the law is above you. The grounds cited by her in the application were not at all mitigating circumstances to have granted dispensation of personal appearance. To say the least, that was a ploy adopted to circumvent the due process of law.”
As the judges concluded their decision, they added:
“In our view, the petitioner has raised many justifiable and reasonable apprehensions of miscarriage of justice and likelihood of bias, which would require our interference.”

That is, the Supreme Court judges themselves said that the petitioner, the Professor, had raised justifiable and substantial apprehensions that justice might be diverted and administered with bias, requiring the Court's intervention.
In accepting our Professor's petition to transfer Jayalalithaa's disproportionate-assets case to a court in another State, the Supreme Court stated all this and announced the following conclusions:

1. “The Government headed by Selvi Jayalalithaa has, with mala fide intention and with the deliberate assistance of the Public Prosecutor, interfered in C.C. No. 7 of 1997 in order to undermine a fair and independent trial.”

2. “The Public Prosecutor failed in his duty by making no attempt to cross-examine or re-examine the witnesses who changed their earlier statements. This establishes beyond doubt that the prosecution secretly decided and acted to pave the way for the acquittal of all the accused, including Jayalalithaa, and thereby interfered with the administration of justice.”

3. “The Public Prosecutor's consent to Chief Minister Selvi J. Jayalalithaa being exempted from personal appearance under Section 313 of the Code of Criminal Procedure while she was in Chennai shows that he was not acting independently, but according to the wishes of the first accused, Selvi Jayalalithaa.”

4. “Chief Minister Jayalalithaa's unlawful interference in C.C. No. 7 of 1997, and the Public Prosecutor's unlawful assistance to the accused, violated Article 21 of the Constitution of India and Section 313 of the Code of Criminal Procedure. Above all, it constituted a grave attempt to subvert the criminal justice system.”
The other important directions in the Supreme Court judgment were as follows:

“Since the Court finds sufficient prima facie evidence in the case brought by the DMK, there is a possibility that justice will not be secured if the case is tried in Tamil Nadu. It is therefore a fit case for transfer to another State.”
“The Chief Justice of the Karnataka High Court, in consultation with the Karnataka Government, shall establish a Special Court in Bengaluru under the Prevention of Corruption Act within six weeks.”
“A senior advocate experienced in criminal cases shall be appointed Public Prosecutor, and a junior advocate shall also be appointed to assist him.”

“The Public Prosecutor appointed by the Chief Justice shall recall and re-examine the prosecution witnesses who had turned hostile, and shall also initiate separate criminal proceedings against witnesses who deliberately changed their testimony.”
“If witnesses face threats, the Karnataka Government shall provide them adequate police protection.”
“All the accused shall appear without fail when questioned, and the case shall be heard daily.”
“The Tamil Nadu Government shall immediately send all documents connected with the case to the Special Court.”

The Supreme Court delivered this judgment on 18 November 2003. When reporters asked for my reaction, I said: “The judgment delivered is precisely of the kind we sought. From the Supreme Court judges' examination of our request and their judgment, it has been proved that the proceedings in Tamil Nadu were conducted contrary to justice and law. I am confident that this Supreme Court judgment will help secure justice through a fair process. Jayalalithaa must accept moral responsibility.”

PMK founder Dr Ramadoss and P. Chidambaram, then General Secretary of the Congress Jananayaga Peravai and now Union Finance Minister, also demanded that Jayalalithaa resign immediately as Chief Minister.

The Supreme Court's direction that the case be tried speedily and heard every day was issued on 18 November 2003. The AIADMK was then in power in Tamil Nadu. Do you know when the Chennai Special Court issued the formal order transferring the case to Bengaluru in compliance with the Supreme Court's order? On 10 September 2004, ten months later. Does this not show how lethargically the AIADMK government acted even upon the Supreme Court's direction?

Ten years have passed since then, and the case is still not over. The reason is nothing other than the accused obtaining adjournments one after another, entering every nook and cranny of the law on various pretexts, with the intention of delaying the trial.
(To be continued)
With affection,
M.K.
